audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 8 draft aligned to Samuel's corrupt sons, elders' request for monarchy, Divine response, royal extraction warning, and authorization to appoint a king; later source/witness audit required.
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew-first naming, restored/mystical separation, narrative ambiguity, institutional power, vulnerable persons, violence, and misuse risks.

verses.restored: It happened when Samuel grew old that he set his sons as judges for Israel.
Name of his firstborn son was Joel, and name of his second Abijah; they were judges in Beer-sheba.
His sons did not walk in his ways. They turned aside after gain, took bribes, and bent justice.
All elders of Israel gathered and came to Samuel at Ramah.
They said to him, Look, you have grown old, and your sons do not walk in your ways. Now set for us a king to judge us like all nations.
The thing was evil in eyes of Samuel when they said, Give us a king to judge us. Samuel prayed to YHWH.
YHWH said to Samuel, Listen to voice of people in all they say to you, for not you have they rejected; rather, me they have rejected from being king over them.
According to all deeds they have done from day I brought them up from Egypt to this day—forsaking me and serving other gods—so they are doing also to you.
Now listen to their voice. Only solemnly warn them and declare to them justice/custom of king who will reign over them.
Samuel spoke all words of YHWH to people asking from him a king.
He said, This will be justice/custom of king who will reign over you: your sons he will take and place for himself in his chariots and among his horsemen, and they will run before his chariot.
He will set for himself commanders of thousands and commanders of fifties, and some to plow his plowing and reap his harvest, and to make his weapons of war and equipment of his chariots.
Your daughters he will take for perfumers, cooks, and bakers.
Your fields, vineyards, and olive groves, the best, he will take and give to his servants.
Your seed and vineyards he will tithe and give to his eunuchs/officials and servants.
Your male servants, female servants, best young men, and donkeys he will take and use for his work.
Your flocks he will tithe, and you yourselves will become his servants.
You will cry out in that day because of your king whom you chose for yourselves, but YHWH will not answer you in that day.
The people refused to listen to voice of Samuel. They said, No, but a king shall be over us.
Then we also will be like all nations, and our king will judge us, go out before us, and fight our battles.
Samuel heard all words of people and spoke them in ears of YHWH.
YHWH said to Samuel, Listen to their voice and make a king reign for them. Samuel said to humans of Israel, Go, each to his city.

translation_notes: Samuel's sons take bribes and bend justice. Hereditary appointment and family reputation do not protect institutions from accountability.
The elders' request arises partly from real judicial corruption and succession failure. Their grievances should not be dismissed merely because monarchy is dangerous.
Samuel experiences the request personally, while YHWH reframes it. Leaders must not confuse criticism of their governance with rejection of God.

The chapter's 'justice/custom of the king' is an anti-authoritarian catalogue of extraction: conscription, militarization, forced labor, land seizure, taxation, appropriation of workers and animals, and eventual servitude.
'He will take' is the chapter's repeated political verb. State legitimacy does not make every taking just.
Sons and daughters are both conscripted into gendered royal labor. The warning rejects treating young people as resources of rulers.
Eunuchs/officials in verse 15 should not be stigmatized; the critique concerns royal patronage and extraction, not bodies or gender variance.
The warning that YHWH will not answer in that day is not permission to abandon people suffering under governments they once supported.
The people's desire to be like other nations includes security, visible leadership, and defense; political fear can coexist with poor institutional choices.
YHWH tells Samuel to listen and appoint a king. Permission within narrative tension is not unqualified endorsement of monarchy, authoritarianism, nationalism, or leader worship.
The anti-monarchical warning applies also to modern religious, corporate, and political leaders who centralize wealth, labor, land, and violence.
Nothing in this chapter authorizes antisemitic claims that Jews rejected God. This is an internal Jewish scripture debating Israelite governance, preserved within Jewish tradition.
Mystical readings must not turn Divine kingship into theocracy, clerical rule, or suppression of democratic accountability.
safety_note: Maximum anti-authoritarian safeguard: do not turn Divine kingship into theocracy, monarchy, nationalism, clerical rule, or leader worship. Do not dismiss the people's real experience of corrupt judges. The chapter exposes extraction through conscription, forced labor, land seizure, taxation, militarization, and servitude. Never weaponize it antisemitically.
